title: Waves, Light & Sound
objective: Students will explore waves, light & sound concepts including Light & Seeing in the Dark, Light & Sound Vocabulary, Communication with Light & Sound and more.

steps.content: Let's discover the amazing world of Light & Seeing in the Dark!
Observe that objects in darkness can be seen only when illuminated by a light source
Let's practice together! Explain that we need light to see objects — without any light source, it is completely dark
If you go into a completely dark room with you, can they explain why they can't see anything until a torch is switched on?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Light & Seeing in the Dark: Explain that we need light to see objects — without any light source, it is completely dark Describe an observation showing objects become visible only when light reaches them
Let's explore Light & Sound Vocabulary! Have you ever wondered about this?

Name and use vocabulary for how light and sound behave — light source, transparent, translucent, opaque, shadow, reflect, vibration, pitch, volume — and apply these terms correctly when describing observations about how light travels and how sounds are made and changed
Let's practice together! Correctly classify a set of materials as transparent, translucent, or opaque and explain each term
If you held up a piece of frosted glass, could you say whether it's transparent, translucent, or opaque — and explain what that word means?

Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Light & Sound Vocabulary. Correctly classify a set of materials as transparent, translucent, or opaque and explain each term Explain what causes a shadow using the words 'opaque' and 'light source' correctly
Today we're going to learn about Communication with Light & Sound. Get ready for an adventure!
Design and build a device that uses light or sound to solve the problem of communicating over a distance
Let me show you something cool. Design a device that uses light or sound to communicate over a distance (e.g. string telephone, torch signals)
Could you design a simple device — like a string telephone or a mirror signalling system — to send a message to someone far away?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Communication with Light & Sound: Design a device that uses light or sound to communicate over a distance (e.g. string telephone, torch signals) Build and test the device, identifying what works and what could be improved
Have you ever thought about Vibrations & Sound? Let's find out together!
Understand that vibrating materials can make sound, and that sound can make materials vibrate
Let's practice together! Describe that sounds are made when objects vibrate (move back and forth quickly)
Can you pluck a rubber band and explain that the vibrating band makes the sound, and then hold a ruler on a speaker and see it shake from the music?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Vibrations & Sound: Describe that sounds are made when objects vibrate (move back and forth quickly) Give at least three examples of vibrating objects making sound (drum skin, guitar string, voice box)
Let's discover the amazing world of Transparent, Translucent & Opaque!
Investigate the effect of placing objects made of different materials in the path of a beam of light, discovering transparent, translucent, and opaque materials
Let's practice together! Test at least six materials and classify them as transparent, translucent, or opaque
Can you test different materials with a torch and sort them into ones light goes through, ones that block light, and ones in between?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Transparent, Translucent & Opaque: Test at least six materials and classify them as transparent, translucent, or opaque Define transparent (light passes through clearly), translucent (some light passes, blurry), opaque (no light passes)
